title: Quality and Technical Agreements Explained
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A quality technical agreement defines GMP and GDP responsibilities between parties. Learn what to include, how to negotiate one, and the pitfalls inspectors find.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette set against deep navy, with a single subtle green accent. Two professionals in a calm, modern quality office review a crisp printed document on a polished surface, a fountain pen resting beside it and a softly blurred laboratory and glass partition in the background. Soft natural side light catches the paper and a sliver of clean glassware; shallow depth of field keeps the document sharp while the room melts into smooth bokeh. Composed at 4:3, premium and editorial in feel, conveying trust, precision and partnership. No text, no logos, no lettering of any kind.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a quality agreement and a supply contract?
faqs.a: A supply contract governs commercial terms such as price, volumes, lead times and penalties. A quality technical agreement is a separate document that defines GMP and GDP responsibilities like batch release, deviation handling, change control and data integrity. Keeping them apart means your quality terms are not reopened every time commercial conditions change, and it satisfies the EU GMP Chapter 7 expectation for a clear written agreement on outsourced activities.

faqs.q: Who should sign a quality technical agreement?
faqs.a: It should be signed by people with genuine authority over the quality obligations on each side, typically the heads of quality or named QP, RP or RPi functions, rather than procurement alone. Reference roles instead of named individuals so the agreement remains valid through staff changes. Signatories should understand and be able to deliver the responsibilities they are committing to.

faqs.q: How often should a quality technical agreement be reviewed?
faqs.a: Set a defined periodic review cycle when the agreement is drafted and assign clear ownership for it. Beyond the scheduled review, update the document whenever the supplied activity, site, product or regulatory status changes. Inspectors frequently flag agreements that are years old and no longer reflect the actual process, so a current, accurate document is a strong compliance signal.
